mission.title: Install and Configure Thanos on Kubernetes
mission.description: Thanos is a highly available metrics system that extends existing Prometheus deployments with long-term storage, global query view, and unlimited retention across multiple clusters. A complete Thanos deployment runs the Query, Query Frontend, Compact, and Store Gateway components against an S3-compatible object storage bucket. This mission installs all four core components and explains how to wire them to AWS S3, GCS, or Azure Blob Storage for production use.

steps.title: Add the stevehipwell Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the community-maintained Thanos Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration. This chart pulls images directly from the upstream 'quay.io/thanos/thanos' registry.
```bash
helm repo add stevehipwell https://steve.hipwell.xyz/helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Thanos with all production components enabled
steps.description:
Install Thanos in a new namespace called 'thanos'. Query and Store Gateway are on by default. Compact and Query Frontend must be enabled explicitly. The chart's default objstoreConfig uses an on-disk filesystem path so the components come up cleanly on a fresh cluster, and the next step swaps it for real object storage before any data needs to be persisted.
```bash
helm install thanos stevehipwell/thanos --version 1.23.0 \
  --namespace thanos --create-namespace \
  --set compact.enabled=true \
  --set queryFrontend.enabled=true
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that all four core component pods are running successfully. You should see thanos-query, thanos-query-frontend, thanos-compact, and thanos-store-gateway.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace thanos
kubectl get statefulsets,deployments --namespace thanos
```

steps.title: Configure production object storage
steps.description:
Thanos requires S3-compatible object storage for durable long-term retention. The chart's default filesystem path is only suitable for getting components up. Create an objstore.yml for your cloud provider and apply it as a chart override. The chart will create a Secret named 'thanos-objstore' with the contents.

AWS S3 example (objstore.yml):
```yaml
type: S3
config:
  bucket: my-thanos-metrics
  endpoint: s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
  access_key: <ACCESS_KEY>
  secret_key: <SECRET_KEY>
```
GCS example:
```yaml
type: GCS
config:
  bucket: my-thanos-metrics
  service_account: |-
    <contents of service-account.json>
```
Azure Blob example:
```yaml
type: AZURE
config:
  storage_account: mystorageaccount
  storage_account_key: <KEY>
  container: thanos-metrics
```
Apply by upgrading the release with the new objstoreConfig:
```bash
helm upgrade thanos stevehipwell/thanos --version 1.23.0 \
  --namespace thanos \
  --reuse-values \
  --set-file objstoreConfig.value=objstore.yml
```
The Compact and Store Gateway pods will restart and reconnect to the new bucket.

steps.title: Enable persistence on Compact and Store Gateway
steps.description:
Persistence is disabled by default to allow a clean install on clusters without a StorageClass. For real workloads, enable PVCs so Compact and Store Gateway keep block metadata across restarts.
```bash
helm upgrade thanos stevehipwell/thanos --version 1.23.0 \
  --namespace thanos \
  --reuse-values \
  --set compact.persistence.enabled=true \
  --set storeGateway.persistence.enabled=true
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Set resource limits on the components that do the heavy lifting (Compact, Store Gateway, Query). Tune to match your cluster size and ingest rate.
```bash
helm upgrade thanos stevehipwell/thanos --version 1.23.0 \
  --namespace thanos \
  --reuse-values \
  --set query.resources.limits.cpu=500m \
  --set query.resources.limits.memory=1Gi \
  --set compact.resources.limits.cpu=1 \
  --set compact.resources.limits.memory=2Gi \
  --set storeGateway.resources.limits.cpu=500m \
  --set storeGateway.resources.limits.memory=2Gi
```

steps.title: Access the Thanos Query UI
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to the Query Frontend service and open the Thanos UI. The Query Frontend caches and splits long-range queries before sending them on to Query.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/thanos-query-frontend --namespace thanos 10902:10902
```
Open http://localhost:10902. The Stores tab should list the connected store-gateway and any sidecars or remote endpoints you've configured.

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Thanos Helm release from the cluster. This deletes the deployments, statefulsets, services, and the chart-managed objstore secret.
```bash
helm uninstall thanos --namespace thanos
```

uninstall.title: Clean up persistent volume claims
uninstall.description:
If you enabled persistence on Compact, Store Gateway, Receive, or Rule, the PVCs are retained by default ('persistence.retainDeleted: true' in the chart). Delete them explicitly when you want the storage released.
```bash
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace thanos
```

uninstall.title: Empty the object storage bucket
uninstall.description:
Helm uninstall does not touch your S3/GCS/Azure bucket. If you no longer need the historical metric blocks, empty the bucket via your cloud provider's CLI or console. For AWS S3:
```bash
aws s3 rm s3://my-thanos-metrics --recursive
```
For GCS:
```bash
gsutil -m rm -r gs://my-thanos-metrics/**
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'thanos' namespace and verify it has been removed.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace thanos
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current values and objstore config
upgrade.description:
Export the current Helm values and the objstore secret before upgrading.
```bash
helm get values thanos --namespace thanos > thanos-backup.yaml
kubectl get secret thanos-objstore -n thanos -o yaml > thanos-objstore-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Refresh the stevehipwell Helm repository to pick up the latest chart.
```bash
helm repo update
helm search repo stevehipwell/thanos --versions | head -5
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Thanos
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the release to the desired chart version. The Thanos blocks format is forward-compatible across recent versions, but always review the chart and Thanos release notes for any breaking changes before upgrading production.
```bash
helm upgrade thanos stevehipwell/thanos --namespace thanos --version 1.23.0 --reuse-values
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that all components rolled out and are running the new image.
```bash
kubectl rollout status deploy/thanos-query -n thanos
kubectl rollout status deploy/thanos-query-frontend -n thanos
kubectl rollout status statefulset/thanos-store-gateway -n thanos
kubectl rollout status statefulset/thanos-compact -n thanos
kubectl get pods -n thanos -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].image}' | tr ' ' '\n' | sort -u
```

troubleshooting.title: Compact or Store Gateway in CrashLoopBackOff after enabling object storage
troubleshooting.description:
The most common cause is a malformed or unreachable objstore config. Check the pod logs for the parsed config and any S3/GCS/Azure error.
```bash
kubectl logs -n thanos statefulset/thanos-compact --tail=100
kubectl logs -n thanos statefulset/thanos-store-gateway --tail=100
kubectl get secret thanos-objstore -n thanos -o jsonpath='{.data.config}' | base64 -d
```
Verify the bucket exists, the credentials are valid, and the IAM policy allows s3:GetObject, s3:PutObject, s3:DeleteObject, and s3:ListBucket on the bucket and its objects.

troubleshooting.title: Query UI shows no stores
troubleshooting.description:
If the Stores tab in the Query UI is empty, the Query component has not discovered the Store Gateway. The chart enables DNS service discovery by default. Confirm the Store Gateway service exists and is reachable.
```bash
kubectl get svc -n thanos thanos-store-gateway-headless
kubectl exec -n thanos deploy/thanos-query -c thanos -- wget -qO- http://localhost:10902/api/v1/stores
```

troubleshooting.title: No metrics from existing Prometheus
troubleshooting.description:
Thanos serves historical metrics from object storage and live metrics from sidecars or remote-write Receive endpoints. If queries return no data, ensure each Prometheus instance has a Thanos sidecar uploading blocks to the same bucket, and that the sidecar's StoreAPI is reachable from Query. Add the sidecar endpoints to query.additionalEndpoints in the chart values.
```bash
kubectl exec -n thanos deploy/thanos-query -c thanos -- wget -qO- http://localhost:10902/api/v1/stores
```

troubleshooting.title: Image pull errors referencing 'docker.io/bitnami'
troubleshooting.description:
Older Thanos install instructions used the Bitnami chart, whose default image registry was migrated to 'docker.io/bitnamilegacy/' in September 2025 and is no longer updated. This mission uses the community 'stevehipwell/thanos' chart, which pulls directly from 'quay.io/thanos/thanos'. If you see ImagePullBackOff errors mentioning 'docker.io/bitnami/thanos', confirm you are following this mission's helm command and not an older Bitnami-based snippet.
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace thanos | grep -A2 Image
```

troubleshooting.title: Compact running but blocks not being downsampled
troubleshooting.description:
Compact processes blocks once they're old enough (default 40h for the first downsampling level, 10d for the second). For a freshly-deployed cluster you won't see downsampled data immediately. Confirm Compact is healthy and is reading blocks from the bucket.
```bash
kubectl logs -n thanos statefulset/thanos-compact --tail=200 | grep -E 'compaction|downsample'
kubectl exec -n thanos statefulset/thanos-compact -- wget -qO- http://localhost:10902/metrics | grep thanos_compact
```

troubleshooting.title: High memory usage in Store Gateway
troubleshooting.description:
Store Gateway caches block index data in memory. On large buckets this can grow significantly. Tune 'storeGateway.extraArgs' with --index-cache-size and --chunk-pool-size, or increase the resource limits.
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace thanos
```

resolution.summary: A successful production install of Thanos has the Query, Query Frontend, Compact, and Store Gateway pods all Running in the 'thanos' namespace, an 'objstore.yml' Secret pointing at an S3, GCS, or Azure bucket, and the Query UI reachable on the Query Frontend service. With persistence enabled and resource limits set per component, the deployment is ready to ingest blocks from Prometheus sidecars or Thanos Receive and serve historical queries against the bucket.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add stevehipwell https://steve.hipwell.xyz/helm-charts
helm repo update

helm install thanos stevehipwell/thanos --version 1.23.0 \
  --namespace thanos --create-namespace \
  --set compact.enabled=true \
  --set queryFrontend.enabled=true

helm upgrade thanos stevehipwell/thanos --version 1.23.0 \
  --namespace thanos --reuse-values \
  --set-file objstoreConfig.value=objstore.yml

helm upgrade thanos stevehipwell/thanos --version 1.23.0 \
  --namespace thanos --reuse-values \
  --set compact.persistence.enabled=true \
  --set storeGateway.persistence.enabled=true

prerequisites.description: Production Thanos requires an S3-compatible object storage bucket (AWS S3, GCS, Azure Blob, or any S3-compatible store such as MinIO or Ceph RGW) with credentials that can read, write, list, and delete objects. A default StorageClass is recommended once persistence is enabled on Compact and Store Gateway. Helm and kubectl must be installed locally.
